g.data-narration: Welcome to the topic of Finite, Infinite, and Bipartite Graphs. We'll explore classification of graphs and their key properties for students.
g.text: Finite, Infinite, and Bipartite Graphs

g.data-narration: A finite graph has a countable number of vertices and edges. It can be represented using adjacency matrices and is used in practical applications like networks.
g.text: 1. Finite Graphs
A finite graph has countable vertices and edges. Properties: |V| and |E| are finite positive integers.
Can use adjacency matrices (n × n). Applications: social networks, road networks, computer topologies.
Complete graph Kn has n vertices with n(n-1)/2 edges connecting every pair.

g.data-narration: Infinite graphs contain infinitely many vertices or edges. Countably infinite graphs map to natural numbers, while uncountably infinite graphs use real numbers.
g.text: 2. Infinite Graphs
Contains infinite number of vertices or edges. Types: Countably Infinite (mapped to ℕ) and Uncountably Infinite (like ℝ).
Applications: infinite state systems in automata theory, theoretical complexity analysis, infinite networks and lattices.
For courses, finite graphs are the primary focus; infinite graphs provide theoretical foundation.

g.data-narration: A bipartite graph has vertices in two disjoint sets U and V where edges only connect vertices from different sets. It can be 2-colored with no odd cycles.
g.text: 3. Bipartite Graphs
Vertex set partitioned into two disjoint sets U and V. Every edge connects vertex in U to vertex in V.
Key properties: No edge within same partition, can be 2-colored, contains no odd cycles.
Characterization Theorem: Graph is bipartite if and only if it contains no odd-length cycle.

g.data-narration: Complete bipartite graph K m,n has m vertices in one set connected to all n vertices in the other set. It has m plus n vertices and m times n edges.
g.text: 4. Complete Bipartite Graphs (Km,n)
Every vertex in set of size m connects to every vertex in set of size n. No edges within partitions.
Properties: Vertices = m + n, Edges = m × n. Regular only when m = n. K1,n is called a Star graph.
Example: K3,4 has 7 vertices and 12 edges. Not regular since degrees are 4 and 3.

g.data-narration: Important theorems: bipartite equals 2-colorable, no odd cycles means bipartite. K n,n is n-regular. Star graph K 1,n has n plus one vertices and n edges.
g.text: 5. Key Theorems and Formulas
Theorem 1: Graph is bipartite if and only if it is 2-colorable. Theorem 2: No odd cycle = bipartite.
Complete graph Kn: edges = n(n-1)/2. Complete bipartite Km,n: vertices = m+n, edges = m×n.
Special cases: Kn,n is n-regular. Star K1,n has n+1 vertices and n edges.

g.data-narration: Common exam tips: bipartite means no odd cycles, K m,n has m n edges, star is K 1 n, and K n n is n-regular. Practice identifying bipartitions from diagrams.
g.text: 6. Exam Tips and Common Mistakes
Tips: Bipartite ⇔ No odd cycles (frequently tested). Km,n: vertices = m+n, edges = m×n. Kn: edges = n(n-1)/2.
Star graph K1,n has n+1 vertices and n edges. Kn,n is n-regular. Bipartite ⇔ 2-colorable.
Avoid: Confusing Kn with Km,n, forgetting edges in Km,n = m×n (not m+n), assuming odd cycles only matter.
Practice: Identify bipartition from diagrams, count vertices and edges for Km,n problems.
